facebook.texto: Discover a light, low-calorie gelatin recipe inspired by a popular Japanese home routine. The Gelatin Trick Celebs Quietly Rely On — You Won’t Believe Why I didn’t expect something this simple to make people stare

analise_criativa.avatar: Woman named Sofia Duarte, appearing in profile picture as a blonde woman in a casual green top, likely mid-40s. Ad image shows gelatin/food with a UGC-style testimonial text overlay quoting weight loss from 338 to 134 lbs.
analise_criativa.editing: Static image with food visual (yellow gelatin/mango dessert) combined with a handwritten-style testimonial text bubble overlay. Persona-driven page with a real face as the advertiser avatar. Low production, native feel.
analise_criativa.observation: The main creative image is still food-focused (gelatin dessert) without a full body transformation or real person in the main frame — however the profile avatar of Sofia Duarte is a real person and the testimonial overlay adds a human social proof element. The extreme weight claim (338 to 134 lbs) may raise compliance concerns in UK/DE markets.
analise_criativa.highlight: Strong curiosity hook combining celebrity angle ('Gelatin Trick Celebs Quietly Rely On'), Japanese routine authority framing, and a shocking testimonial number. The phrase 'make people stare' triggers vanity and social validation. Worth testing the copy angle with a stronger before/after visual of a real person.

facebook.texto: Discover a light, low-calorie gelatin recipe inspired by a popular Japanese home routine. The Gelatin Trick Celebs Quietly Rely On — You Won’t Believe Why I didn’t expect something this simple to make people stare

analise_criativa.avatar: Same Sofia Duarte persona — blonde woman in profile picture, mid-40s, casual appearance. Ad image shows yellow gelatin dessert with a different testimonial text overlay quoting weight loss from 208 to 134 lbs.
analise_criativa.editing: Static image with food visual and testimonial text bubble overlay, identical format to AD 3 but with a different testimonial variant. Native, low-production UGC feel designed to blend into the feed.
analise_criativa.observation: This is a variant split-test of AD 3 using a different testimonial number (208 to 134 vs 338 to 134). Both use the same copy and page. The more modest claim here (208 to 134) may be more credible and compliant. Both library IDs are distinct but share the same base creative ID (2209897396411889 as parent).
analise_criativa.highlight: A/B testimonial number test is a smart credibility lever. The 208→134 version feels more believable than the 338→134 and could yield better CTR from skeptical audiences. Worth isolating this variable in a structured test.

facebook.texto: Discover a light, low-calorie gelatin recipe inspired by a popular Japanese home routine. The Gelatin Trick Celebs Quietly Rely On — You Won’t Believe Why I didn’t expect something this simple to make people stare

analise_criativa.avatar: Sofia Duarte persona — same blonde woman profile picture. Ad image appears identical to AD 4 with the same 208 to 134 testimonial overlay and yellow gelatin visual.
analise_criativa.editing: Static image, identical visual format and testimonial to AD 4. Separate library ID suggests this is a duplicate running under a different ad ID, possibly for budget distribution or audience segmentation purposes.
analise_criativa.observation: This creative appears to be a near-exact duplicate of AD 4 running simultaneously under a different library ID (1370055925023093 vs 1747491663097797). Same text, same image, same page. Could indicate a scaling strategy through duplicated ad sets rather than creative variation.
analise_criativa.highlight: Running identical creatives under multiple IDs is a common scaling tactic to bypass frequency fatigue across ad sets. If performance data is available per ID, this could reveal which audience segments respond best to the same creative.

repeated_creatives.description: Ads 2 and 3 share identical visual creative — same yellow gelatin dessert image with the same '208 to 134 lbs' testimonial text overlay — and the exact same ad copy, avatar (Sofia Duarte), keyword, format, and run duration. These are duplicate creatives likely being split-tested across different ad sets or audiences.

All three ads (1, 2, 3) share the same avatar persona (Sofia Duarte), identical headline and body copy, same keyword, same format, and same 17-day run duration, indicating they originate from the same campaign with only minor image/testimonial number variations as the differentiator.

frequent_avatars.type: Relatable mid-40s woman — blonde, casual appearance, UGC-style persona presenting as a real person sharing a personal weight loss discovery rather than a brand spokesperson. Designed to build trust through social proof and first-person authenticity.

facebook_cross_analysis.dominant_mechanism: The 'Gelatin Trick' — a simple, low-calorie food-based ritual framed as a Japanese-inspired home routine that passively supports weight loss, positioned as a secret used by celebrities.
facebook_cross_analysis.emerging_angle: Japanese home routine framing — anchoring a Western weight loss product or recipe to Japanese cultural authority signals a fresh geo-cultural credibility angle that borrows from the broader 'Eastern wellness secret' trend but applies it specifically to an everyday accessible food (gelatin), which feels novel and low-barrier.
facebook_cross_analysis.market_signal: The US market is currently testing food-ritual-based weight loss hooks with heavy social proof via dramatic before/after testimonial numbers, using UGC-style female personas to mask advertorial content and drive curiosity clicks around simple, celebrity-adjacent 'tricks'.
